title: Neuron

infobox.value: ([American English](/wiki/American_English "American English")), **neurone** ([British English](/wiki/British_English "British English")),[[1]](#cite_note-1) or **nerve cell**, is a [cell](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") that is [excitable](/wiki/Membrane_potential#Cell_excitability "Membrane potential"), firing electric signals called [action potentials](/wiki/Action_potentials "Action potentials") across a [neural network](/wiki/Neural_network_(biology) "Neural network (biology)") in the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system"), mainly in the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system"), and helps to receive and conduct impulses. Neurons communicate with other cells via [synapses](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse"), which are specialized connections that commonly use minute amounts of chemical [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") to pass the electric signal from the presynaptic neuron to the target cell through the synaptic gap.

is the body of the neuron. As it contains the [nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus"), most [protein synthesis](/wiki/Protein_biosynthesis "Protein biosynthesis") occurs here. The nucleus can range from 3 to 18 micrometers in diameter.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

of a neuron are cellular extensions with many branches. This overall shape and structure are referred to metaphorically as a dendritic tree. The branches form fractal patterns that repeat at multiple size scales.[[9]](#cite_note-9) This fractal tree is where the majority of input to the neuron occurs via the [dendritic spine](/wiki/Dendritic_spine "Dendritic spine").

is a finer, cable-like projection that can extend tens, hundreds, or even tens of thousands of times the diameter of the soma in length. The axon primarily carries [nerve signals](/wiki/Nerve_signal "Nerve signal") away from the soma and carries some types of information back to it. Many neurons have only one axon, but this axon may—and usually will—undergo extensive branching, enabling communication with many target cells. The part of the axon where it emerges from the soma is called the **[axon hillock](/wiki/Axon_hillock "Axon hillock")**. Besides being an anatomical structure, the axon hillock also has the greatest density of [voltage-dependent sodium channels](/wiki/Voltage-dependent_sodium_channels "Voltage-dependent sodium channels"). This makes it the most easily excited part of the neuron and the spike initiation zone for the axon. In electrophysiological terms, it has the most negative [threshold potential](/wiki/Threshold_potential "Threshold potential").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Primary cell of the nervous system

This article is about the type of cell. For other uses, see [Neuron (disambiguation)](/wiki/Neuron_(disambiguation) "Neuron (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Neutron](/wiki/Neutron "Neutron").

A **neuron** ([American English](/wiki/American_English "American English")), **neurone** ([British English](/wiki/British_English "British English")),[[1]](#cite_note-1) or **nerve cell**, is a [cell](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") that is [excitable](/wiki/Membrane_potential#Cell_excitability "Membrane potential"), firing electric signals called [action potentials](/wiki/Action_potentials "Action potentials") across a [neural network](/wiki/Neural_network_(biology) "Neural network (biology)") in the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system"), mainly in the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system"), and helps to receive and conduct impulses. Neurons communicate with other cells via [synapses](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse"), which are specialized connections that commonly use minute amounts of chemical [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") to pass the electric signal from the presynaptic neuron to the target cell through the synaptic gap.

Neurons are the main components of [nervous tissue](/wiki/Nervous_tissue "Nervous tissue") in all [animals](/wiki/Animalia "Animalia") except [sponges](/wiki/Sponge "Sponge") and [placozoans](/wiki/Placozoans "Placozoans"). [Plants](/wiki/Plant "Plant") and [fungi](/wiki/Fungi "Fungi") do not have nerve cells. Molecular evidence suggests that the ability to generate electric signals first appeared in evolution some 700 to 800 million years ago, during the [Tonian](/wiki/Tonian "Tonian") period. Predecessors of neurons were the [peptidergic](/wiki/Peptidergic "Peptidergic") secretory cells. They eventually gained new gene modules which enabled cells to create post-synaptic scaffolds and [ion channels](/wiki/Ion_channel "Ion channel") that generate fast electrical signals. The ability to generate electric signals was a key innovation in the evolution of the nervous system.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Neurons are typically classified into three types based on their function. [Sensory neurons](/wiki/Sensory_neuron "Sensory neuron") respond to [stimuli](/wiki/Stimulus_(physiology) "Stimulus (physiology)") such as touch, sound, or light that affect the cells of the [sensory organs](/wiki/Sense "Sense"), and they send signals to the [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord") and then to the sensorial area in the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain"). [Motor neurons](/wiki/Motor_neuron "Motor neuron") receive signals from the brain and spinal cord to control everything from [muscle contractions](/wiki/Muscle_contraction "Muscle contraction")[[3]](#cite_note-3) to [glandular output](/wiki/Gland "Gland"). [Interneurons](/wiki/Interneuron "Interneuron") connect neurons to other neurons within the same region of the brain or spinal cord. When multiple neurons are functionally connected together, they form what is called a [neural circuit](/wiki/Neural_circuit "Neural circuit").

A neuron contains all the structures of other cells such as a [nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus"), [mitochondria](/wiki/Mitochondria "Mitochondria"), and [Golgi bodies](/wiki/Golgi_bodies "Golgi bodies"), but has additional unique structures such as an [axon](/wiki/Axon "Axon"), and [dendrites](/wiki/Dendrite "Dendrite").[[4]](#cite_note-Betts-4) The [soma](/wiki/Soma_(biology) "Soma (biology)") or cell body, is a compact structure, and the axon and dendrites are filaments extruding from the soma. Dendrites typically branch profusely and extend a few hundred micrometers from the soma. The axon leaves the soma at a swelling called the [axon hillock](/wiki/Axon_hillock "Axon hillock") and travels for as far as 1 meter in humans or more in other species. It branches but usually maintains a constant diameter. At the farthest tip of the axon's branches are [axon terminals](/wiki/Axon_terminals "Axon terminals"), where the neuron can transmit a signal across the [synapse](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse") to another cell. Neurons may lack dendrites or have no axons. The term [neurite](/wiki/Neurite "Neurite") is used to describe either a dendrite or an axon, particularly when the cell is [undifferentiated](/wiki/Cellular_differentiation "Cellular differentiation").

Most neurons receive signals via the dendrites and soma and send out signals down the axon. At the majority of synapses, signals cross from the axon of one neuron to the dendrite of another. However, synapses can connect an axon to another axon or a dendrite to another dendrite. The signaling process is partly electrical and partly chemical. Neurons are electrically excitable, due to the maintenance of [voltage](/wiki/Voltage "Voltage") gradients across their [membranes](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane"). If the voltage changes by a large enough amount over a short interval, the neuron generates an [all-or-nothing](/wiki/All-or-none_law "All-or-none law") [electrochemical](/wiki/Electrochemical "Electrochemical") pulse called an [action potential](/wiki/Action_potential "Action potential"). This potential travels rapidly along the axon and activates synaptic connections as it reaches them. Synaptic signals may be [excitatory](/wiki/Excitatory_postsynaptic_potential "Excitatory postsynaptic potential") or [inhibitory](/wiki/Inhibitory_postsynaptic_potential "Inhibitory postsynaptic potential"), increasing or reducing the net voltage that reaches the soma.

In most cases, neurons are generated by [neural stem cells](/wiki/Neural_stem_cell "Neural stem cell") during brain development and childhood. [Neurogenesis](/wiki/Neurogenesis "Neurogenesis") largely ceases during adulthood in most areas of the brain.

## Nervous system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Nervous system")]

Neurons are the primary components of the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system"), along with the [glial cells](/wiki/Glial_cells "Glial cells") that give them structural and metabolic support.[[5]](#cite_note-Moore3-5) The nervous system is made up of the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system"), which includes the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") and [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord"), and the [peripheral nervous system](/wiki/Peripheral_nervous_system "Peripheral nervous system"), which includes the [autonomic](/wiki/Autonomic_nervous_system "Autonomic nervous system"), [enteric](/wiki/Enteric_nervous_system "Enteric nervous system") and [somatic nervous systems](/wiki/Somatic_nervous_system "Somatic nervous system").[[6]](#cite_note-6) In vertebrates, the majority of neurons belong to the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system"), but some reside in peripheral [ganglia](/wiki/Ganglion "Ganglion"), and many sensory neurons are situated in sensory organs such as the [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina") and [cochlea](/wiki/Cochlea "Cochlea").

Axons may bundle into [nerve fascicles](/wiki/Nerve_fascicle "Nerve fascicle") that make up the [nerves](/wiki/Nerve "Nerve") in the peripheral nervous system (like strands of wire that make up a cable). In the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system") bundles of axons are called [nerve tracts](/wiki/Nerve_tract "Nerve tract").

## Anatomy and histology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Anatomy and histology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Complete_neuron_cell_diagram_en.svg/330px-Complete_neuron_cell_diagram_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Complete_neuron_cell_diagram_en.svg)

Diagram of a typical myelinated vertebrate motor neuron

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Anatomy_of_a_Neuron_with_Synapse.png/330px-Anatomy_of_a_Neuron_with_Synapse.png)](/wiki/File:Anatomy_of_a_Neuron_with_Synapse.png)

Schematic of a single [pyramidal neuron](/wiki/Pyramidal_neuron "Pyramidal neuron"), with a [synapse](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse") from an incoming [axon](/wiki/Axon "Axon") onto a [dendritic spine](/wiki/Dendritic_spine "Dendritic spine")

Neurons are highly specialized for the processing and transmission of cellular signals. Given the diversity of functions performed in different parts of the nervous system, there is a wide variety in their shape, size, and electrochemical properties. For instance, the soma of a neuron can vary from 4 to 100 [micrometers](/wiki/Micrometre "Micrometre") in diameter.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

* The **[soma](/wiki/Soma_(biology) "Soma (biology)")** is the body of the neuron. As it contains the [nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus"), most [protein synthesis](/wiki/Protein_biosynthesis "Protein biosynthesis") occurs here. The nucleus can range from 3 to 18 micrometers in diameter.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* The **[dendrites](/wiki/Dendrites "Dendrites")** of a neuron are cellular extensions with many branches. This overall shape and structure are referred to metaphorically as a dendritic tree. The branches form fractal patterns that repeat at multiple size scales.[[9]](#cite_note-9) This fractal tree is where the majority of input to the neuron occurs via the [dendritic spine](/wiki/Dendritic_spine "Dendritic spine").
* The **[axon](/wiki/Axon "Axon")** is a finer, cable-like projection that can extend tens, hundreds, or even tens of thousands of times the diameter of the soma in length. The axon primarily carries [nerve signals](/wiki/Nerve_signal "Nerve signal") away from the soma and carries some types of information back to it. Many neurons have only one axon, but this axon may—and usually will—undergo extensive branching, enabling communication with many target cells. The part of the axon where it emerges from the soma is called the **[axon hillock](/wiki/Axon_hillock "Axon hillock")**. Besides being an anatomical structure, the axon hillock also has the greatest density of [voltage-dependent sodium channels](/wiki/Voltage-dependent_sodium_channels "Voltage-dependent sodium channels"). This makes it the most easily excited part of the neuron and the spike initiation zone for the axon. In electrophysiological terms, it has the most negative [threshold potential](/wiki/Threshold_potential "Threshold potential").
  + While the axon and axon hillock are generally involved in information outflow, this region can also receive input from other neurons.
* The **[axon terminal](/wiki/Axon_terminal "Axon terminal")** is found at the end of the axon farthest from the soma and contains [synapses](/wiki/Synapses "Synapses"). Synaptic boutons are specialized structures where [neurotransmitter](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") chemicals are released to communicate with target neurons. In addition to synaptic boutons at the axon terminal, a neuron may have [en passant boutons](/wiki/En_passant_boutons "En passant boutons"), which are located along the length of the axon.

The accepted view of the neuron attributes dedicated functions to its various anatomical components; however, dendrites and axons often act in ways contrary to their so-called main function.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Axons and dendrites in the central nervous system are typically only about one micrometer thick, while some in the peripheral nervous system are much thicker. The soma is usually about 10–25 micrometers in diameter and often is not much larger than the cell nucleus it contains. The longest axon of a human [motor neuron](/wiki/Motor_neuron "Motor neuron") can be over a meter long, reaching from the base of the spine to the toes.

Sensory neurons can have axons that run from the toes to the [posterior column](/wiki/Posterior_column "Posterior column") of the spinal cord, over 1.5 meters in adults. [Giraffes](/wiki/Giraffe "Giraffe") have single axons several meters in length running along the entire length of their necks. Much of what is known about axonal function comes from studying the [squid giant axon](/wiki/Squid_giant_axon "Squid giant axon"), an ideal experimental preparation because of its relatively immense size (0.5–1 millimeter thick, several centimeters long).

Fully differentiated neurons are permanently [postmitotic](/wiki/G0_phase "G0 phase")[[10]](#cite_note-10) however, stem cells present in the adult brain may regenerate functional neurons throughout the life of an organism (see [neurogenesis](/wiki/Neurogenesis "Neurogenesis")). [Astrocytes](/wiki/Astrocyte "Astrocyte") are star-shaped [glial cells](/wiki/Glial_cell "Glial cell") that have been observed to turn into neurons by virtue of their stem cell-like characteristic of [pluripotency](/wiki/Pluripotency "Pluripotency").[[11]](#cite_note-Talifu-11)

### Membrane

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Membrane")]

Like all animal cells, the [cell body](/wiki/Soma_(biology) "Soma (biology)") of every neuron is enclosed by a [plasma membrane](/wiki/Plasma_membrane "Plasma membrane"), a bilayer of [lipid](/wiki/Lipid "Lipid") molecules with many types of embedded protein structures.[[12]](#cite_note-12) A [lipid bilayer](/wiki/Lipid_bilayer "Lipid bilayer") is a powerful electrical [insulator](/wiki/Insulator_(electricity) "Insulator (electricity)"), but in neurons, many of the protein structures embedded in the membrane are electrically active. These include [ion channels](/wiki/Ion_channel "Ion channel") that permit electrically charged ions to flow across the membrane and ion pumps that chemically transport ions from one side of the membrane to the other. Most ion channels are [gated](/wiki/Gating_(electrophysiology) "Gating (electrophysiology)"), permeable only to specific types of ions. Some ion channels are [voltage gated](/wiki/Voltage-gated_ion_channel "Voltage-gated ion channel"), meaning that they can be switched between open and closed states by altering the voltage difference across the membrane. Others are chemically gated, meaning that they can be switched between open and closed states by interactions with chemicals that diffuse through the extracellular fluid. The [ions](/wiki/Ion "Ion") include [sodium](/wiki/Sodium "Sodium"), [potassium](/wiki/Potassium "Potassium"), [chloride](/wiki/Chloride "Chloride"), and [calcium](/wiki/Calcium "Calcium"). The interactions between ion channels and [ion pumps](/wiki/Ion_transporter "Ion transporter") produce a voltage difference across the membrane, typically a little less than 1/10 of a [volt](/wiki/Volt "Volt") at baseline. This voltage has two functions: first, it provides a power source for an assortment of voltage-dependent protein machineries that are embedded in the membrane; second, it provides a basis for electrical signal transmission between different parts of the membrane.

### Histology and internal structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Histology and internal structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Gyrus_Dentatus_40x.jpg/250px-Gyrus_Dentatus_40x.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gyrus_Dentatus_40x.jpg)

[Golgi-stained](/wiki/Golgi_stain "Golgi stain") neurons in human [hippocampal](/wiki/Hippocampal "Hippocampal") tissue

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/SUM_110913_Cort_Neurons_2.5d_in_vitro_488_Phalloidin_no_perm_4_cmle-2.png/250px-SUM_110913_Cort_Neurons_2.5d_in_vitro_488_Phalloidin_no_perm_4_cmle-2.png)](/wiki/File:SUM_110913_Cort_Neurons_2.5d_in_vitro_488_Phalloidin_no_perm_4_cmle-2.png)

Actin filaments in a mouse cortical neuron in culture

Numerous microscopic clumps called [Nissl bodies](/wiki/Nissl_body "Nissl body") (or Nissl substance) are seen when nerve cell bodies are stained with a basophilic ("base-loving") dye. These structures consist of [rough endoplasmic reticulum](/wiki/Endoplasmic_reticulum#Rough_endoplasmic_reticulum "Endoplasmic reticulum") and associated [ribosomal RNA](/wiki/Ribosomal_RNA "Ribosomal RNA"). Named after German psychiatrist and neuropathologist [Franz Nissl](/wiki/Franz_Nissl "Franz Nissl") (1860–1919), they are involved in protein synthesis and their prominence can be explained by the fact that nerve cells are very metabolically active. Basophilic dyes such as [aniline](/wiki/Aniline "Aniline") or (weakly) [hematoxylin](/wiki/Hematoxylin "Hematoxylin")[[13]](#cite_note-13) highlight negatively charged components, and so bind to the phosphate backbone of the ribosomal RNA.

The cell body of a neuron is supported by a complex mesh of structural proteins called [neurofilaments](/wiki/Neurofilament "Neurofilament"), which together with [neurotubules](/wiki/Neurotubule "Neurotubule") (neuronal microtubules) are assembled into larger neurofibrils.[[14]](#cite_note-Webster-14) Some neurons also contain pigment granules, such as [neuromelanin](/wiki/Neuromelanin "Neuromelanin") (a brownish-black pigment that is byproduct of synthesis of [catecholamines](/wiki/Catecholamine "Catecholamine")), and [lipofuscin](/wiki/Lipofuscin "Lipofuscin") (a yellowish-brown pigment), both of which accumulate with age.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) Other structural proteins that are important for neuronal function are [actin](/wiki/Actin "Actin") and the [tubulin](/wiki/Tubulin "Tubulin") of [microtubules](/wiki/Microtubule "Microtubule"). [Class III β-tubulin](/wiki/Class_III_%CE%B2-tubulin "Class III β-tubulin") is found almost exclusively in neurons. Actin is predominately found at the tips of axons and dendrites during neuronal development. There the actin dynamics can be modulated via an interplay with microtubule.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

There are different internal structural characteristics between axons and dendrites. Typical axons seldom contain [ribosomes](/wiki/Ribosomes "Ribosomes"), except some in the initial segment. Dendrites contain granular endoplasmic reticulum or ribosomes, in diminishing amounts as the distance from the cell body increases.

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Classification")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/GFPneuron.png/250px-GFPneuron.png)](/wiki/File:GFPneuron.png)

Image of pyramidal neurons in mouse [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex") expressing [green fluorescent protein](/wiki/Green_fluorescent_protein "Green fluorescent protein"). The red staining indicates [GABAergic](/wiki/GABA "GABA") interneurons.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

See also: [List of distinct cell types in the adult human body § Nervous system](/wiki/List_of_distinct_cell_types_in_the_adult_human_body#Nervous_system "List of distinct cell types in the adult human body")

Neurons vary in shape and size and can be classified by their [morphology](/wiki/Morphology_(biology) "Morphology (biology)") and function.[[20]](#cite_note-Al-20) The anatomist [Camillo Golgi](/wiki/Camillo_Golgi "Camillo Golgi") grouped neurons into two types; type I with long axons used to move signals over long distances and type II with short axons, which can often be confused with dendrites. Type I cells can be further classified by the location of the soma. The basic morphology of type I neurons, represented by spinal [motor neurons](/wiki/Motor_neurons "Motor neurons"), consists of a cell body called the soma and a long thin axon covered by a [myelin sheath](/wiki/Myelin_sheath "Myelin sheath"). The dendritic tree wraps around the cell body and receives signals from other neurons. The end of the axon has branching [axon terminals](/wiki/Axon_terminal "Axon terminal") that release neurotransmitters into a gap called the [synaptic cleft](/wiki/Synaptic_cleft "Synaptic cleft") between the terminals and the dendrites of the next neuron.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Structural classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Structural classification")]

#### Polarity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Polarity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Structural_classification_of_neurons_by_polarity_hariadhi.svg/250px-Structural_classification_of_neurons_by_polarity_hariadhi.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Structural_classification_of_neurons_by_polarity_hariadhi.svg)

Different kinds of neurons

Most neurons can be anatomically characterized as:[[4]](#cite_note-Betts-4)

* [Unipolar](/wiki/Unipolar_neuron "Unipolar neuron"): single process. Unipolar cells are exclusively sensory neurons. Their dendrites receive sensory information, sometimes directly from the stimulus itself. The cell bodies of unipolar neurons are always found in ganglia. Sensory reception is a peripheral function, so the cell body is in the periphery, though closer to the CNS in a ganglion. The axon projects from the dendrite endings, past the cell body in a ganglion, and into the central nervous system.
* [Bipolar](/wiki/Bipolar_cell "Bipolar cell"): 1 axon and 1 dendrite. They are found mainly in the [olfactory epithelium](/wiki/Olfactory_epithelium "Olfactory epithelium"), and as part of the retina.
* [Multipolar](/wiki/Multipolar_neuron "Multipolar neuron"): 1 axon and 2 or more dendrites
  + [Golgi I](/wiki/Golgi_I "Golgi I"): neurons with long-projecting axonal processes; examples are pyramidal cells, Purkinje cells, and anterior horn cells
  + [Golgi II](/wiki/Golgi_II "Golgi II"): neurons whose axonal process projects locally; the best example is the granule cell
* [Anaxonic](/wiki/Anaxonic_neuron "Anaxonic neuron"): where the axon cannot be distinguished from the dendrite(s)
* [Pseudounipolar](/wiki/Pseudounipolar_cells "Pseudounipolar cells"): 1 process which then serves as both an axon and a dendrite

#### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other")]

Some unique neuronal types can be identified according to their location in the nervous system and distinct shape. Some examples are:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Basket cells](/wiki/Basket_cell "Basket cell"), interneurons that form a dense plexus of terminals around the soma of target cells, found in the cortex and [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum")
* [Betz cells](/wiki/Betz_cell "Betz cell"), large motor neurons in [primary motor cortex](/wiki/Primary_motor_cortex "Primary motor cortex")
* [Lugaro cells](/wiki/Lugaro_cell "Lugaro cell"), interneurons of the cerebellum
* [Medium spiny neurons](/wiki/Medium_spiny_neuron "Medium spiny neuron"), most neurons in the [corpus striatum](/wiki/Corpus_striatum "Corpus striatum")
* [Purkinje cells](/wiki/Purkinje_cell "Purkinje cell"), huge neurons in the cerebellum, a type of Golgi I multipolar neuron
* [Pyramidal cells](/wiki/Pyramidal_cell "Pyramidal cell"), neurons with triangular soma, a type of Golgi I
* [Rosehip cells](/wiki/Rosehip_neuron "Rosehip neuron"), unique human inhibitory neurons that interconnect with Pyramidal cells
* [Renshaw cells](/wiki/Renshaw_cell "Renshaw cell"), neurons with both ends linked to [alpha motor neurons](/wiki/Alpha_motor_neuron "Alpha motor neuron")
* [Unipolar brush cells](/wiki/Unipolar_brush_cell "Unipolar brush cell"), interneurons with unique dendrite ending in a brush-like tuft
* [Granule cells](/wiki/Granule_cell "Granule cell"), a type of Golgi II neuron
* [Anterior horn](/wiki/Anterior_horn_(spinal_cord) "Anterior horn (spinal cord)") cells, [motoneurons](/wiki/Motoneurons "Motoneurons") located in the spinal cord
* [Spindle cells](/wiki/Spindle_neuron "Spindle neuron"), interneurons that connect widely separated areas of the brain

### Functional classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Functional classification")]

#### Direction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Direction")]

* [Afferent neurons](/wiki/Afferent_neuron "Afferent neuron") convey information from tissues and organs into the central nervous system and are also called [sensory neurons](/wiki/Sensory_neurons "Sensory neurons").
* [Efferent neurons](/wiki/Efferent_neuron "Efferent neuron") (motor neurons) transmit signals from the central nervous system to the effector cells.
* [Interneurons](/wiki/Interneuron "Interneuron") connect neurons within specific regions of the central nervous system.

Afferent and efferent also refer generally to neurons that, respectively, bring information to or send information from the brain.

#### Action on other neurons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Action on other neurons")]

A neuron affects other neurons by releasing a neurotransmitter that binds to [chemical receptors](/wiki/Receptor_(biochemistry) "Receptor (biochemistry)"). The effect on the postsynaptic neuron is determined by the type of receptor that is activated, not by the presynaptic neuron or by the neurotransmitter. Receptors are classified broadly as *excitatory* (causing an increase in firing rate), *inhibitory* (causing a decrease in firing rate), or *modulatory* (causing long-lasting effects not directly related to firing rate).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The two most common (90%+) neurotransmitters in the brain, [glutamate](/wiki/Glutamate "Glutamate") and [GABA](/wiki/GABA "GABA"), have largely consistent actions. Glutamate acts on several types of receptors and has effects that are excitatory at [ionotropic receptors](/wiki/Ionotropic_receptor "Ionotropic receptor") and a modulatory effect at [metabotropic receptors](/wiki/Metabotropic_receptor "Metabotropic receptor"). Similarly, GABA acts on several types of receptors, but all of them have inhibitory effects (in adult animals, at least). Because of this consistency, it is common for neuroscientists to refer to cells that release glutamate as "excitatory neurons", and cells that release GABA as "inhibitory neurons". Some other types of neurons have consistent effects, for example, "excitatory" motor neurons in the spinal cord that release [acetylcholine](/wiki/Acetylcholine "Acetylcholine"), and "inhibitory" [spinal neurons](/wiki/Spinal_neuron "Spinal neuron") that release [glycine](/wiki/Glycine "Glycine").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The distinction between excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters is not absolute. Rather, it depends on the class of chemical receptors present on the postsynaptic neuron. In principle, a single neuron, releasing a single neurotransmitter, can have excitatory effects on some targets, inhibitory effects on others, and modulatory effects on others still. For example, [photoreceptor cells](/wiki/Photoreceptor_cell "Photoreceptor cell") in the retina constantly release the neurotransmitter glutamate in the absence of light. So-called OFF [bipolar cells](/wiki/Retinal_bipolar_cells "Retinal bipolar cells") are, like most neurons, excited by the released glutamate. However, neighboring target neurons called ON bipolar cells are instead inhibited by glutamate, because they lack typical [ionotropic](/wiki/Ionotropic_receptor "Ionotropic receptor") [glutamate receptors](/wiki/Glutamate_receptors "Glutamate receptors") and instead express a class of inhibitory [metabotropic](/wiki/Metabotropic_receptor "Metabotropic receptor") glutamate receptors.[[21]](#cite_note-21) When light is present, the photoreceptors cease releasing glutamate, which relieves the ON bipolar cells from inhibition, activating them; this simultaneously removes the excitation from the OFF bipolar cells, silencing them.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

It is possible to identify the type of inhibitory effect a presynaptic neuron will have on a postsynaptic neuron, based on the proteins the presynaptic neuron expresses. [Parvalbumin](/wiki/Parvalbumin "Parvalbumin")-expressing neurons typically dampen the output signal of the postsynaptic neuron in the [visual cortex](/wiki/Visual_cortex "Visual cortex"), whereas [somatostatin](/wiki/Somatostatin "Somatostatin")-expressing neurons typically block dendritic inputs to the postsynaptic neuron.[[22]](#cite_note-pmid22878717-22)

#### Discharge patterns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Discharge patterns")]

Neurons have intrinsic electroresponsive properties like intrinsic transmembrane voltage [oscillatory](/wiki/Neural_oscillation "Neural oscillation") patterns.[[23]](#cite_note-llinas2014-23) So neurons can be classified according to their [electrophysiological](/wiki/Electrophysiology "Electrophysiology") characteristics:

* Tonic or regular spiking. Some neurons are typically constantly (tonically) active, typically firing at a constant frequency. Example: interneurons in [neurostriatum](/wiki/Striatum "Striatum").
* Phasic or [bursting](/wiki/Bursting "Bursting"). Neurons that fire in bursts are called phasic.
* Fast-spiking. Some neurons are notable for their high firing rates, for example, some types of cortical inhibitory interneurons, cells in [globus pallidus](/wiki/Globus_pallidus "Globus pallidus"), [retinal ganglion cells](/wiki/Retinal_ganglion_cells "Retinal ganglion cells").[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

#### Neurotransmitter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Neurotransmitter")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Neurotransmitters.jpg/250px-Neurotransmitters.jpg)](/wiki/File:Neurotransmitters.jpg)

Synaptic vesicles containing neurotransmitters

Main article: [Neurotransmitter](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter")

[Neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") are chemical messengers passed from one neuron to another neuron or to a [muscle cell](/wiki/Muscle_cell "Muscle cell") or [gland cell](/wiki/Gland "Gland").

* Cholinergic neurons – acetylcholine. [Acetylcholine](/wiki/Acetylcholine "Acetylcholine") is released from presynaptic neurons into the synaptic cleft. It acts as a [ligand](/wiki/Ligand "Ligand") for both ligand-gated ion channels and [metabotropic](/wiki/Metabotropic_receptor "Metabotropic receptor") (GPCRs) [muscarinic receptors](/wiki/Muscarinic_acetylcholine_receptor "Muscarinic acetylcholine receptor"). [Nicotinic receptors](/wiki/Nicotinic_receptors "Nicotinic receptors") are pentameric ligand-gated ion channels composed of alpha and beta subunits that bind [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine"). Ligand binding opens the channel causing the influx of [Na+](/wiki/Sodium "Sodium") depolarization and increases the probability of presynaptic neurotransmitter release. Acetylcholine is synthesized from [choline](/wiki/Choline "Choline") and [acetyl coenzyme A](/wiki/Acetyl_coenzyme_A "Acetyl coenzyme A").
* Adrenergic neurons – noradrenaline. [Noradrenaline](/wiki/Noradrenaline "Noradrenaline") (norepinephrine) is released from most [postganglionic](/wiki/Postganglionic "Postganglionic") neurons in the [sympathetic nervous system](/wiki/Sympathetic_nervous_system "Sympathetic nervous system") onto two sets of GPCRs: [alpha adrenoceptors](/wiki/Adrenergic_receptor "Adrenergic receptor") and [beta adrenoceptors](/wiki/Beta_adrenoceptor "Beta adrenoceptor"). Noradrenaline is one of the three common [catecholamine](/wiki/Catecholamine "Catecholamine") neurotransmitters, and the most prevalent of them in the [peripheral nervous system](/wiki/Peripheral_nervous_system "Peripheral nervous system"); as with other catecholamines, it is synthesized from [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine").
* GABAergic neurons – [gamma aminobutyric acid](/wiki/Gamma_aminobutyric_acid "Gamma aminobutyric acid"). GABA is one of two neuroinhibitors in the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system") (CNS), along with glycine. GABA has a homologous function to [ACh](/wiki/Acetylcholine "Acetylcholine"), gating anion channels that allow [Cl−](/wiki/Chlorine "Chlorine") ions to enter the post synaptic neuron. Cl− causes hyperpolarization within the neuron, decreasing the probability of an action potential firing as the voltage becomes more negative (for an action potential to fire, a positive voltage threshold must be reached). GABA is synthesized from glutamate neurotransmitters by the enzyme [glutamate decarboxylase](/wiki/Glutamate_decarboxylase "Glutamate decarboxylase").
* Glutamatergic neurons – glutamate. [Glutamate](/wiki/Glutamate "Glutamate") is one of two primary excitatory amino acid neurotransmitters, along with [aspartate](/wiki/Aspartic_acid "Aspartic acid"). Glutamate receptors are one of four categories, three of which are ligand-gated ion channels and one of which is a [G-protein](/wiki/G_protein "G protein") coupled receptor (often referred to as GPCR).

:   1. [AMPA](/wiki/AMPA "AMPA") and [Kainate](/wiki/Kainic_acid "Kainic acid") receptors function as [cation](/wiki/Ion "Ion") channels permeable to Na+ cation channels mediating fast excitatory synaptic transmission.
    2. [NMDA](/wiki/N-Methyl-D-aspartic_acid "N-Methyl-D-aspartic acid") receptors are another cation channel that is more permeable to [Ca2+](/wiki/Calcium_in_biology "Calcium in biology"). The function of NMDA receptors depends on glycine receptor binding as a co-[agonist](/wiki/Agonist "Agonist") within the channel pore. NMDA receptors do not function without both ligands present.
    3. Metabotropic receptors, GPCRs modulate synaptic transmission and postsynaptic excitability.

    :   Glutamate can cause excitotoxicity when blood flow to the brain is interrupted, resulting in [brain damage](/wiki/Brain_damage "Brain damage"). When blood flow is suppressed, glutamate is released from presynaptic neurons, causing greater NMDA and AMPA receptor activation than normal outside of stress conditions, leading to elevated Ca2+ and Na+ entering the post synaptic neuron and cell damage. Glutamate is synthesized from the amino acid glutamine by the enzyme [glutamate synthase](/wiki/Glutamine_oxoglutarate_aminotransferase "Glutamine oxoglutarate aminotransferase").

* Dopaminergic neurons—[dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine"). [Dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") is a neurotransmitter that acts on D1 type (D1 and D5) Gs-coupled receptors, which increase cAMP and PKA, and D2 type (D2, D3, and D4) receptors, which activate Gi-coupled receptors that decrease cAMP and PKA. Dopamine is connected to mood and behavior and modulates both pre- and post-synaptic neurotransmission. Loss of dopamine neurons in the [substantia nigra](/wiki/Substantia_nigra "Substantia nigra") has been linked to [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"). Dopamine is synthesized from the amino acid [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine"). Tyrosine is catalyzed into levodopa (or [L-DOPA](/wiki/L-DOPA "L-DOPA")) by [tyrosine hydroxylase](/wiki/Tyrosine_hydroxylase "Tyrosine hydroxylase"), and levodopa is then converted into dopamine by the aromatic amino acid [decarboxylase](/wiki/Carboxy-lyases "Carboxy-lyases").
* Serotonergic neurons—[serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin"). [Serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") (5-Hydroxytryptamine, 5-HT) can act as excitatory or inhibitory. Of its four 5-HT receptor classes, 3 are GPCR and 1 is a ligand-gated cation channel. Serotonin is synthesized from [tryptophan](/wiki/Tryptophan "Tryptophan") by [tryptophan hydroxylase](/wiki/Tryptophan_hydroxylase "Tryptophan hydroxylase"), and then further by decarboxylase. A lack of 5-HT at postsynaptic neurons has been linked to depression. Drugs that block the presynaptic [serotonin transporter](/wiki/Serotonin_transporter "Serotonin transporter") are used for treatment, such as [Prozac](/wiki/Prozac "Prozac") and [Zoloft](/wiki/Zoloft "Zoloft").
* Purinergic neurons—ATP. [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") is a neurotransmitter acting at both ligand-gated ion channels ([P2X](/wiki/P2X "P2X") receptors) and GPCRs ([P2Y](/wiki/P2Y_receptor "P2Y receptor")) receptors. ATP is, however, best known as a [cotransmitter](/wiki/Cotransmitter "Cotransmitter"). Such [purinergic signaling](/wiki/Purinergic_signaling "Purinergic signaling") can also be mediated by other [purines](/wiki/Purine "Purine") like [adenosine](/wiki/Adenosine "Adenosine"), which particularly acts at P2Y receptors.
* Histaminergic neurons—[histamine](/wiki/Histamine "Histamine"). [Histamine](/wiki/Histamine "Histamine") is a [monoamine neurotransmitter](/wiki/Monoamine_neurotransmitter "Monoamine neurotransmitter") and [neuromodulator](/wiki/Neuromodulator "Neuromodulator"). Histamine-producing neurons are found in the [tuberomammillary nucleus](/wiki/Tuberomammillary_nucleus "Tuberomammillary nucleus") of the [hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Histamine is involved in [arousal](/wiki/Arousal "Arousal") and regulating sleep/wake behaviors.

#### Multimodel classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Multimodel classification")]

Since 2012 there has been a push from the cellular and [computational neuroscience](/wiki/Computational_neuroscience "Computational neuroscience") community to come up with a universal classification of neurons that will apply to all neurons in the brain as well as across species. This is done by considering the three essential qualities of all neurons: electrophysiology, morphology, and the individual transcriptome of the cells. Besides being universal this classification has the advantage of being able to classify astrocytes as well. A method called [patch-sequencing](/wiki/Patch-sequencing "Patch-sequencing") in which all three qualities can be measured at once is used extensively by the [Allen Institute for Brain Science](/wiki/Allen_Institute_for_Brain_Science "Allen Institute for Brain Science").[[27]](#cite_note-27) In 2023, a comprehensive cell atlas of the adult, and developing human brain at the transcriptional, epigenetic, and functional levels was created through an international collaboration of researchers using the most cutting-edge molecular biology approaches.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Connectivity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Connectivity")]

Main articles: [Synapse](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse") and [Chemical synapse](/wiki/Chemical_synapse "Chemical synapse")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Chemical_synapse_schema_cropped.jpg/500px-Chemical_synapse_schema_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chemical_synapse_schema_cropped.jpg)

A signal propagating down an axon to the cell body and dendrites of the next cell

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Neuro_Muscular_Junction.png/250px-Neuro_Muscular_Junction.png)](/wiki/File:Neuro_Muscular_Junction.png)

Chemical synapse

Neurons communicate with each other via [synapses](/wiki/Synapses "Synapses"), where either the [axon terminal](/wiki/Axon_terminal "Axon terminal") of one cell contacts another neuron's dendrite, soma, or, less commonly, axon. Neurons such as Purkinje cells in the cerebellum can have over 1000 dendritic branches, making connections with tens of thousands of other cells; other neurons, such as the magnocellular neurons of the [supraoptic nucleus](/wiki/Supraoptic_nucleus "Supraoptic nucleus"), have only one or two dendrites, each of which receives thousands of synapses.

Synapses can be excitatory or inhibitory, either increasing or decreasing activity in the target neuron, respectively. Some neurons also communicate via electrical synapses, which are direct, electrically conductive [junctions](/wiki/Gap_junction "Gap junction") between cells.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

When an action potential reaches the axon terminal, it opens [voltage-gated calcium channels](/wiki/Voltage-dependent_calcium_channel "Voltage-dependent calcium channel"), allowing [calcium ions](/wiki/Calcium_in_biology "Calcium in biology") to enter the terminal. Calcium causes [synaptic vesicles](/wiki/Synaptic_vesicles "Synaptic vesicles") filled with neurotransmitter molecules to fuse with the membrane, releasing their contents into the synaptic cleft. The neurotransmitters diffuse across the synaptic cleft and activate receptors on the postsynaptic neuron. High cytosolic calcium in the [axon terminal](/wiki/Axon_terminal "Axon terminal") triggers mitochondrial calcium uptake, which, in turn, activates mitochondrial [energy metabolism](/wiki/Energy_metabolism "Energy metabolism") to produce [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") to support continuous neurotransmission.[[30]](#cite_note-pmid23746507-30)

An [autapse](/wiki/Autapse "Autapse") is a synapse in which a neuron's axon connects to its dendrites.

The [human brain](/wiki/Human_brain "Human brain") has some 8.6 x 1010 (eighty six billion) neurons.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Allen2022-32) Each neuron has on average 7,000 synaptic connections to other neurons. It has been estimated that the brain of a three-year-old child has about 1015 synapses (1 quadrillion). [This number declines with age](/wiki/Synaptic_pruning "Synaptic pruning"), stabilizing by adulthood. Estimates vary for an adult, ranging from 1014 to 5 x 1014 synapses (100 to 500 trillion).[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Nonelectrochemical signaling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Nonelectrochemical signaling")]

Beyond electrical and chemical signaling, studies suggest neurons in healthy human brains can also communicate through:

* force generated by the enlargement of dendritic spines[[34]](#cite_note-34)
* the transfer of [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") – transneuronally transported proteins (TNTPs)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

They can also get modulated by input from the environment and [hormones](/wiki/Hormone "Hormone") released from other parts of the organism,[[37]](#cite_note-37) which could be influenced more or less directly by neurons. This also applies to [neurotrophins](/wiki/Neurotrophin "Neurotrophin") such as [BDNF](/wiki/BDNF "BDNF"). The [gut microbiome](/wiki/Gut_microbiome "Gut microbiome") is also connected with the brain.[[38]](#cite_note-38)
Neurons also communicate with [microglia](/wiki/Microglia "Microglia"), the brain's main immune cells via specialized contact sites, called "somatic junctions". These connections enable microglia to constantly monitor and regulate neuronal functions, and exert neuroprotection when needed.[[39]](#cite_note-Science-39)

## Mechanisms for propagating action potentials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Mechanisms for propagating action potentials")]

Main article: [Action potential](/wiki/Action_potential "Action potential")

In 1937 [John Zachary Young](/wiki/John_Zachary_Young "John Zachary Young") suggested that the [squid giant axon](/wiki/Squid_giant_axon "Squid giant axon") could be used to study neuronal electrical properties.[[40]](#cite_note-40) It is larger than but similar to human neurons, making it easier to study. By inserting electrodes into the squid giant axons, accurate measurements were made of the [membrane potential](/wiki/Membrane_potential "Membrane potential").

The cell membrane of the axon and soma contain voltage-gated ion channels that allow the neuron to generate and propagate an electrical signal (an action potential). Some neurons also generate [subthreshold membrane potential oscillations](/wiki/Subthreshold_membrane_potential_oscillations "Subthreshold membrane potential oscillations"). These signals are generated and propagated by charge-carrying [ions](/wiki/Ions "Ions") including sodium (Na+), potassium (K+), chloride (Cl−), and [calcium (Ca2+)](/wiki/Calcium_signaling "Calcium signaling").

Several stimuli can activate a neuron leading to electrical activity, including [pressure](/wiki/Mechanoreceptor "Mechanoreceptor"), stretch, chemical transmitters, and changes in the electric potential across the cell membrane.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Stimuli cause specific ion-channels within the cell membrane to open, leading to a flow of ions through the cell membrane, changing the membrane potential. Neurons must maintain the specific electrical properties that define their neuron type.[[42]](#cite_note-Harris-Warrick-42)

Thin neurons and axons require less [metabolic](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism") expense to produce and carry action potentials, but thicker axons convey impulses more rapidly. To minimize metabolic expense while maintaining rapid conduction, many neurons have insulating sheaths of [myelin](/wiki/Myelin "Myelin") around their axons. The sheaths are formed by [glial](/wiki/Glia "Glia") cells: [oligodendrocytes](/wiki/Oligodendrocyte "Oligodendrocyte") in the central nervous system and [Schwann cells](/wiki/Schwann_cell "Schwann cell") in the peripheral nervous system. The sheath enables action potentials to travel [faster](/wiki/Saltatory_conduction "Saltatory conduction") than in unmyelinated axons of the same diameter, whilst using less energy. The myelin sheath in peripheral nerves normally runs along the axon in sections about 1 mm long, punctuated by unsheathed [nodes of Ranvier](/wiki/Node_of_Ranvier "Node of Ranvier"), which contain a high density of voltage-gated ion channels. [Multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis") is a neurological disorder that results from the demyelination of axons in the central nervous system.

Some neurons do not generate action potentials but instead generate a [graded electrical signal](/wiki/Graded_potential "Graded potential"), which in turn causes graded neurotransmitter release. Such [non-spiking neurons](/wiki/Non-spiking_neurons "Non-spiking neurons") tend to be sensory neurons or interneurons, because they cannot carry signals long distances.

## Neural coding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Neural coding")]

[Neural coding](/wiki/Neural_coding "Neural coding") is concerned with how sensory and other information is represented in the brain by neurons. The main goal of studying neural coding is to characterize the relationship between the [stimulus](/wiki/Stimulus_(physiology) "Stimulus (physiology)") and the individual or [ensemble](/wiki/Neural_ensemble "Neural ensemble") neuronal responses and the relationships among the electrical activities of the neurons within the ensemble.[[43]](#cite_note-Brown-43) It is thought that neurons can encode both [digital](/wiki/Digital_data "Digital data") and [analog](/wiki/Analog_signal "Analog signal") information.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## All-or-none principle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: All-or-none principle")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/All-or-none_law_en.svg/330px-All-or-none_law_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:All-or-none_law_en.svg)

As long as the stimulus reaches the threshold, the full response will be given. A larger stimulus does not result in a larger response, and vice versa.[[45]](#cite_note-Kalat,_James_W-2016-45): 31

Main article: [All-or-none law](/wiki/All-or-none_law "All-or-none law")

The conduction of nerve impulses is an example of an [all-or-none](/wiki/All-or-none_law "All-or-none law") response. In other words, if a neuron responds at all, then it must respond completely. Greater intensity of stimulation, like brighter image/louder sound, does not produce a stronger signal but can increase firing frequency.[[45]](#cite_note-Kalat,_James_W-2016-45): 31 Receptors respond in different ways to stimuli. Slowly adapting or [tonic receptors](/wiki/Tonic_(physiology) "Tonic (physiology)") respond to a steady stimulus and produce a steady rate of firing. Tonic receptors most often respond to increased stimulus intensity by increasing their firing frequency, usually as a power function of stimulus plotted against impulses per second. This can be likened to an intrinsic property of light where greater intensity of a specific frequency (color) requires more photons, as the photons cannot become "stronger" for a specific frequency.

Other receptor types include quickly adapting or phasic receptors, where firing decreases or stops with a steady stimulus; examples include [skin](/wiki/Human_skin "Human skin") which, when touched causes neurons to fire, but if the object maintains even pressure, the neurons stop firing. The neurons of the skin and muscles that are responsive to pressure and vibration have filtering accessory structures that aid their function.

The [pacinian corpuscle](/wiki/Pacinian_corpuscle "Pacinian corpuscle") is one such structure. It has concentric layers like an onion, which form around the axon terminal. When pressure is applied and the corpuscle is deformed, mechanical stimulus is transferred to the axon, which fires. If the pressure is steady, the stimulus ends; thus, these neurons typically respond with a transient depolarization during the initial deformation and again when the pressure is removed, which causes the corpuscle to change shape again. Other types of adaptation are important in extending the function of several other neurons.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Although neurons have long been assumed to always give a stereotyped maximal response or none at all, there is a body of research that argues that this is only partially correct, and that while it is true that Neurons either fire an Action Potential or do not, the amplitude and duration of the Action Potentials that a Neuron fires can vary greatly, allowing the Neuron to encode information in at least the strength of the Action Potential. Additionally, the analog information carried in the Action Potential has been shown to be able to survive and travel distances originally not thought to be possible. This has been proposed to be a highly effective way to encode information compared to the usual rate and temporal coding theories commonly seen in the literature, with the ability to transfer around 4 times more information than current wisdom would suggest.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

## Etymology and spelling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Etymology and spelling")]

The German anatomist [Heinrich Wilhelm Waldeyer](/wiki/Heinrich_Wilhelm_Gottfried_von_Waldeyer-Hartz "Heinrich Wilhelm Gottfried von Waldeyer-Hartz") introduced the term *neuron* in 1891,[[52]](#cite_note-finger-52) based on the [ancient Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") νεῦρον *neuron* 'sinew, cord, nerve'.[[53]](#cite_note-oed-53)

The word was adopted in French with the spelling *neurone*. That spelling was also used by many writers in English,[[54]](#cite_note-Mehta-54) but has now become rare in American usage and uncommon in British usage.[[55]](#cite_note-ngram-55)[[53]](#cite_note-oed-53)

Some previous works used *nerve cell* (*cellule nervose*), as adopted in [Camillo Golgi](/wiki/Camillo_Golgi "Camillo Golgi")'s 1873 paper on the discovery of the silver staining technique used to visualize nervous tissue under light microscopy.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [History of neuroscience](/wiki/History_of_neuroscience "History of neuroscience")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Golgi_Hippocampus.jpg/250px-Golgi_Hippocampus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Golgi_Hippocampus.jpg)

Drawing by Camillo Golgi of a [hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus") stained using the [silver nitrate](/wiki/Silver_nitrate "Silver nitrate") method

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Purkinje_cell_by_Cajal.png/250px-Purkinje_cell_by_Cajal.png)](/wiki/File:Purkinje_cell_by_Cajal.png)

Drawing of a Purkinje cell in the [cerebellar cortex](/wiki/Cerebellar_cortex "Cerebellar cortex") done by [Santiago Ramón y Cajal](/wiki/Santiago_Ram%C3%B3n_y_Cajal "Santiago Ramón y Cajal"), demonstrating the ability of Golgi's staining method to reveal fine detail

The neuron's place as the primary functional unit of the nervous system was first recognized in the late 19th century through the work of the Spanish anatomist [Santiago Ramón y Cajal](/wiki/Santiago_Ram%C3%B3n_y_Cajal "Santiago Ramón y Cajal").[[57]](#cite_note-López-Muñoz-57)

To make the structure of individual neurons visible, Ramón y Cajal improved a [silver staining process](/wiki/Golgi%27s_method "Golgi's method") that had been developed by [Camillo Golgi](/wiki/Camillo_Golgi "Camillo Golgi").[[57]](#cite_note-López-Muñoz-57) The improved process involves a technique called "double impregnation" and is still in use.

In 1888 Ramón y Cajal published a paper about the bird cerebellum. In this paper, he stated that he could not find evidence for [anastomosis](/wiki/Anastomosis "Anastomosis") between axons and dendrites and called each nervous element "an autonomous canton."[[57]](#cite_note-López-Muñoz-57)[[52]](#cite_note-finger-52) This became known as the [neuron doctrine](/wiki/Neuron_doctrine "Neuron doctrine"), one of the central tenets of modern [neuroscience](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience").[[57]](#cite_note-López-Muñoz-57)

In 1891, the German anatomist [Heinrich Wilhelm Waldeyer](/wiki/Heinrich_Wilhelm_Gottfried_von_Waldeyer-Hartz "Heinrich Wilhelm Gottfried von Waldeyer-Hartz") wrote a highly influential review of the neuron doctrine in which he introduced the term *neuron* to describe the anatomical and physiological unit of the nervous system.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

The silver impregnation stains are a useful method for [neuroanatomical](/wiki/Neuroanatomy "Neuroanatomy") investigations because, for reasons unknown, it stains only a small percentage of cells in a tissue, exposing the complete micro structure of individual neurons without much overlap from other cells.[[60]](#cite_note-Grant-60)

### Neuron doctrine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Neuron doctrine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/PurkinjeCell.jpg/250px-PurkinjeCell.jpg)](/wiki/File:PurkinjeCell.jpg)

Drawing of neurons in the pigeon [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum"), by Spanish neuroscientist [Santiago Ramón y Cajal](/wiki/Santiago_Ram%C3%B3n_y_Cajal "Santiago Ramón y Cajal") in 1899. (A) denotes [Purkinje cells](/wiki/Purkinje_cell "Purkinje cell") and (B) denotes [granule cells](/wiki/Granule_cells "Granule cells"), both of which are multipolar.

The neuron doctrine is the now fundamental idea that neurons are the basic structural and functional units of the nervous system. The theory was put forward by Santiago Ramón y Cajal in the late 19th century. It held that neurons are discrete cells (not connected in a meshwork), acting as metabolically distinct units.

Later discoveries yielded refinements to the doctrine. For example, [glial cells](/wiki/Neuroglia "Neuroglia"), which are non-neuronal, play an essential role in information processing.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Also, electrical synapses are more common than previously thought,[[62]](#cite_note-62) comprising direct, cytoplasmic connections between neurons; In fact, neurons can form even tighter couplings: the squid giant axon arises from the fusion of multiple axons.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Ramón y Cajal also postulated the Law of Dynamic Polarization, which states that a neuron receives signals at its dendrites and cell body and transmits them, as action potentials, along the axon in one direction: away from the cell body.[[64]](#cite_note-sabb-64) The Law of Dynamic Polarization has important exceptions; dendrites can serve as synaptic output sites of neurons[[65]](#cite_note-65) and axons can receive synaptic inputs.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

### Compartmental modelling of neurons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Compartmental modelling of neurons")]

Although neurons are often described as "fundamental units" of the brain, they perform internal computations. Neurons integrate input within dendrites, and this complexity is lost in models that assume neurons to be a fundamental unit. Dendritic branches can be modeled as spatial compartments, whose activity is related to passive membrane properties, but may also be different depending on input from synapses. [Compartmental modelling of dendrites](/wiki/Compartmental_modelling_of_dendrites "Compartmental modelling of dendrites") is especially helpful for understanding the behavior of neurons that are too small to record with electrodes, as is the case for *Drosophila melanogaster*.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

## Neurons in the brain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Neurons in the brain")]

The number of neurons in the brain varies dramatically from species to species.[[68]](#cite_note-nerve_net-68) In a human, there are an estimated 10–20 billion neurons in the [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex") and 55–70 billion neurons in the [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum").[[69]](#cite_note-pmid27187682-69) By contrast, the [nematode](/wiki/Nematode "Nematode") worm *[Caenorhabditis elegans](/wiki/Caenorhabditis_elegans "Caenorhabditis elegans")* has just 302 neurons, making it an ideal [model organism](/wiki/Model_organism "Model organism") as scientists have been able to map all of its neurons. The fruit fly *[Drosophila melanogaster](/wiki/Drosophila_melanogaster "Drosophila melanogaster")*, a common subject in biological experiments, has around 100,000 neurons and exhibits many complex behaviors. Many properties of neurons, from the type of neurotransmitters used to ion channel composition, are maintained across species, allowing scientists to study processes occurring in more complex organisms in much simpler experimental systems.

[Charcot–Marie–Tooth disease](/wiki/Charcot%E2%80%93Marie%E2%80%93Tooth_disease "Charcot–Marie–Tooth disease") (CMT) is a heterogeneous inherited disorder of nerves ([neuropathy](/wiki/Neuropathy "Neuropathy")) that is characterized by loss of muscle tissue and touch sensation, predominantly in the feet and legs extending to the hands and arms in advanced stages. Presently incurable, this disease is one of the most common inherited [neurological disorders](/wiki/Neurological_disorder "Neurological disorder"), affecting 36 in 100,000 people.[[70]](#cite_note-Krajewski-70)

[Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease") (AD), also known simply as *Alzheimer's*, is a [neurodegenerative disease](/wiki/Neurodegenerative_disease "Neurodegenerative disease") characterized by progressive [cognitive](/wiki/Cognitive "Cognitive") deterioration, together with declining activities of daily living and [neuropsychiatric](/wiki/Neuropsychiatric "Neuropsychiatric") symptoms or behavioral changes.[[71]](#cite_note-nihstages-71) The most striking early symptom is loss of short-term memory ([amnesia](/wiki/Amnesia "Amnesia")), which usually manifests as minor forgetfulness that becomes steadily more pronounced with illness progression, with relative preservation of older memories. As the disorder progresses, cognitive (intellectual) impairment extends to the domains of language ([aphasia](/wiki/Aphasia "Aphasia")), skilled movements ([apraxia](/wiki/Apraxia "Apraxia")), and recognition ([agnosia](/wiki/Agnosia "Agnosia")), and functions such as decision-making and planning become impaired.[[72]](#cite_note-BMJ2009-72)[[73]](#cite_note-NEJM2010-73)

[Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease") (PD), also known as *Parkinson's*, is a degenerative disorder of the central nervous system that often impairs motor skills and speech.[[74]](#cite_note-NIH2016-74) Parkinson's disease belongs to a group of conditions called [movement disorders](/wiki/Movement_disorders "Movement disorders").[[75]](#cite_note-75) It is characterized by muscle rigidity, [tremor](/wiki/Tremor "Tremor"), a slowing of physical movement ([bradykinesia](/wiki/Bradykinesia "Bradykinesia")), and in extreme cases, a loss of physical movement ([akinesia](/wiki/Akinesia "Akinesia")). The primary symptoms are the results of decreased stimulation of the [motor cortex](/wiki/Motor_cortex "Motor cortex") by the [basal ganglia](/wiki/Basal_ganglia "Basal ganglia"), normally caused by the insufficient formation and action of dopamine, which is produced in the [dopaminergic neurons](/wiki/Dopaminergic_neurons "Dopaminergic neurons") of the brain. Secondary symptoms may include high-level [cognitive dysfunction](/wiki/Cognitive_dysfunction "Cognitive dysfunction") and subtle language problems. PD is both chronic and progressive.

[Myasthenia gravis](/wiki/Myasthenia_gravis "Myasthenia gravis") is a [neuromuscular disease](/wiki/Neuromuscular_disease "Neuromuscular disease") leading to fluctuating [muscle weakness](/wiki/Muscle_weakness "Muscle weakness") and fatigability during simple activities. Weakness is typically caused by circulating [antibodies](/wiki/Antibodies "Antibodies") that block [acetylcholine receptors](/wiki/Acetylcholine_receptors "Acetylcholine receptors") at the postsynaptic neuromuscular junction, inhibiting the stimulative effect of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine. Myasthenia is treated with [immunosuppressants](/wiki/Immunosuppressants "Immunosuppressants"), [cholinesterase](/wiki/Cholinesterase "Cholinesterase") inhibitors and, in selected cases, [thymectomy](/wiki/Thymectomy "Thymectomy").

### Demyelination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Demyelination")]

Further information: [Demyelinating disease](/wiki/Demyelinating_disease "Demyelinating disease")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Guillain-barr%C3%A9_syndrome_-_Nerve_Damage.gif/250px-Guillain-barr%C3%A9_syndrome_-_Nerve_Damage.gif)](/wiki/File:Guillain-barr%C3%A9_syndrome_-_Nerve_Damage.gif)

[Guillain–Barré syndrome](/wiki/Guillain%E2%80%93Barr%C3%A9_syndrome "Guillain–Barré syndrome") – demyelination

[Demyelination](/wiki/Demyelination "Demyelination") is a process characterized by the gradual loss of the myelin sheath enveloping nerve fibers. When myelin deteriorates, signal conduction along nerves can be significantly impaired or lost, and the nerve eventually withers. Demyelination may affect both central and peripheral nervous systems, contributing to various neurological disorders such as [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis"), [Guillain-Barré syndrome](/wiki/Guillain%E2%80%93Barr%C3%A9_syndrome "Guillain–Barré syndrome"), and [chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy](/wiki/Chronic_inflammatory_demyelinating_polyneuropathy "Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy"). Although demyelination is often caused by an [autoimmune](/wiki/Autoimmunity "Autoimmunity") reaction, it may also be caused by viral infections, metabolic disorders, trauma, and some medications.

### Axonal degeneration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Axonal degeneration")]

Although most injury responses include a calcium influx signaling to promote resealing of severed parts, axonal injuries initially lead to acute [axonal degeneration](/wiki/Axonal_degeneration "Axonal degeneration"), which is the rapid separation of the proximal and distal ends, occurring within 30 minutes of injury.[[76]](#cite_note-NM-76) Degeneration follows with swelling of the [axolemma](/wiki/Axolemma "Axolemma"), and eventually leads to bead-like formation. Granular disintegration of the axonal [cytoskeleton](/wiki/Cytoskeleton "Cytoskeleton") and inner [organelles](/wiki/Organelle "Organelle") occurs after axolemma degradation. Early changes include accumulation of [mitochondria](/wiki/Mitochondria "Mitochondria") in the paranodal regions at the site of injury. The endoplasmic reticulum degrades and mitochondria swell up and eventually disintegrate. The disintegration is dependent on [ubiquitin](/wiki/Ubiquitin "Ubiquitin") and [calpain](/wiki/Calpain "Calpain") [proteases](/wiki/Proteases "Proteases") (caused by the influx of calcium ions), suggesting that axonal degeneration is an active process that produces complete fragmentation. The process takes about roughly 24 hours in the PNS and longer in the CNS. The signaling pathways leading to axolemma degeneration are unknown.

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Development")]

Main article: [Neurogenesis](/wiki/Neurogenesis "Neurogenesis")

Neurons develop through the process of [neurogenesis](/wiki/Neurogenesis "Neurogenesis"), in which [neural stem cells](/wiki/Neural_stem_cell "Neural stem cell") divide to produce [differentiated neurons](/wiki/Cellular_differentiation "Cellular differentiation"). Once fully differentiated they are no longer capable of undergoing [mitosis](/wiki/Mitosis "Mitosis"). Neurogenesis primarily occurs during [embryonic development](/wiki/Embryonic_development "Embryonic development").

Neurons initially develop from the [neural tube](/wiki/Neural_tube "Neural tube") in the embryo. The neural tube has three layers – a [ventricular zone](/wiki/Ventricular_zone "Ventricular zone"), an [intermediate zone](/wiki/Development_of_the_cerebral_cortex "Development of the cerebral cortex"), and a marginal zone. The ventricular zone surrounds the tube's central canal and becomes the [ependyma](/wiki/Ependyma "Ependyma"). Dividing cells of the ventricular zone form the intermediate zone which stretches to the outermost layer of the neural tube called the pial layer. The [gray matter](/wiki/Gray_matter "Gray matter") of the brain is derived from the intermediate zone. The extensions of the neurons in the intermediate zone make up the marginal zone when [myelinated](/wiki/Myelinated "Myelinated") becomes the brain's [white matter](/wiki/White_matter "White matter").[[77]](#cite_note-Caire-77)

Differentiation of the neurons is ordered by their size. Large [motor neurons](/wiki/Motor_neuron "Motor neuron") are first. Smaller sensory neurons together with [glial cell](/wiki/Glial_cell "Glial cell") differentiate at [birth](/wiki/Birth "Birth").[[77]](#cite_note-Caire-77)

[Adult neurogenesis](/wiki/Adult_neurogenesis "Adult neurogenesis") can occur and studies of the age of human neurons suggest that this process occurs only for a minority of cells and that the vast majority of neurons in the [neocortex](/wiki/Neocortex "Neocortex") form before birth and persist without replacement. The extent to which adult neurogenesis exists in humans, and its contribution to cognition are controversial, with conflicting reports published in 2018.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

The body contains a variety of stem cell types that can differentiate into neurons. Researchers found a way to transform human skin cells into nerve cells using [transdifferentiation](/wiki/Transdifferentiation "Transdifferentiation"), in which "cells are forced to adopt new identities".[[79]](#cite_note-twsX33-79)

During [neurogenesis](/wiki/Neurogenesis "Neurogenesis") in the mammalian brain, progenitor and stem cells progress from proliferative divisions to differentiative divisions. This progression leads to the neurons and glia that populate cortical layers. [Epigenetic](/wiki/Epigenetics "Epigenetics") modifications play a key role in regulating [gene expression](/wiki/Gene_expression "Gene expression") in differentiating [neural stem cells](/wiki/Neural_stem_cells "Neural stem cells"), and are critical for cell fate determination in the developing and adult mammalian brain. Epigenetic modifications include [DNA cytosine methylation](/wiki/DNA_methylation "DNA methylation") to form [5-methylcytosine](/wiki/5-methylcytosine "5-methylcytosine") and [5-methylcytosine demethylation](/wiki/DNA_demethylation "DNA demethylation").[[80]](#cite_note-Wang2016-80) [DNA cytosine methylation](/wiki/DNA_methylation "DNA methylation") is catalyzed by [DNA methyltransferases (DNMTs)](/wiki/DNA_methyltransferase "DNA methyltransferase"). Methylcytosine demethylation is catalyzed in several stages by [TET enzymes](/wiki/TET_enzymes "TET enzymes") that carry out oxidative reactions (e.g. [5-methylcytosine](/wiki/5-methylcytosine "5-methylcytosine") to [5-hydroxymethylcytosine](/wiki/5-hydroxymethylcytosine "5-hydroxymethylcytosine")) and enzymes of the DNA [base excision repair](/wiki/Base_excision_repair "Base excision repair") (BER) pathway.[[80]](#cite_note-Wang2016-80)

At different stages of mammalian nervous system development, two DNA repair processes are employed in the repair of DNA double-strand breaks. These pathways are [homologous recombinational](/wiki/Homologous_recombination "Homologous recombination") repair used in proliferating neural precursor cells, and [non-homologous end joining](/wiki/Non-homologous_end_joining "Non-homologous end joining") used mainly at later developmental stages[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Intercellular communication between developing neurons and [microglia](/wiki/Microglia "Microglia") is also indispensable for proper neurogenesis and brain development.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

## Nerve regeneration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuron&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Nerve regeneration")]

Main article: [Neuroregeneration](/wiki/Neuroregeneration "Neuroregeneration")

Peripheral axons can regrow if they are severed,[[83]](#cite_note-Yiu_2006-83) but one neuron cannot be functionally replaced by one of another type ([Llinás' law](/wiki/Llin%C3%A1s%27_law "Llinás' law")).[[23]](#cite_note-llinas2014-23)
